lesson_title: Adding new functionality to a derived class

cards.front: What are the two major ways to construct complex classes in C++?
cards.source: 24.6 - Adding new functionality to a derived class

cards.text: To add new functionality to a derived class, simply {{c1::declare that functionality in the derived class}} like normal.
cards.source: 24.6 - Adding new functionality to a derived class

cards.front: Why might you add functionality to a derived class instead of modifying the base class?
cards.back: You may not have access to the base class source code (e.g., 3rd party library, standard library), or modifying it would be risky (updates could overwrite changes).
cards.source: 24.6 - Adding new functionality to a derived class

cards.question: Will this code compile?
cards.explanation: Base has no getValue() function. Only Derived has it. Base objects cannot access Derived functionality.
cards.source: 24.6 - Adding new functionality to a derived class

cards.text: Because {{c1::Derived}} is-a {{c2::Base}}, Derived has access to stuff in Base. However, {{c2::Base}} does NOT have access to anything in {{c1::Derived}}.
cards.source: 24.6 - Adding new functionality to a derived class

cards.front: Can you add functions to classes from the standard library by deriving from them?
cards.back: Yes, you can inherit from standard library classes and add your own functionality in derived classes, even though you can't modify the standard library code itself.
cards.source: 24.6 - Adding new functionality to a derived class

cards.concept: When to add functionality via inheritance vs modifying base class
cards.explanation:
Add to derived class when:
  • You don't have access to base class source
  • Base class is from 3rd party (updates would overwrite)
  • Base class is part of standard library
  • Base class code is precompiled
Modify base class when:
  • You own the code
  • Functionality applies to all derived classes
examples: Can't modify std::vector, but can derive from it
3rd party library updates would erase your changes
cards.source: 24.6 - Adding new functionality to a derived class

cards.text: Because m_value is declared as {{c1::protected}} in the Base class, the Derived class has {{c2::direct access}} to it.
cards.source: 24.6 - Adding new functionality to a derived class

cards.question: What does this code output?
cards.explanation: Derived can access protected member m_value from Base and return it via getValue().
cards.source: 24.6 - Adding new functionality to a derived class

cards.front: Does a base class have access to functions defined in a derived class?
cards.back: No, base classes have no access to derived class members. The relationship is one-way: derived can access base, but not vice versa.
cards.source: 24.6 - Adding new functionality to a derived class
